title: Architecture: two-phase generator (canonical model + view formatters)
description:
Architectural decision that shapes how .1 / .2 / .3 / .4 and all 18 TCs are implemented.

## Problem
Cross-referential integrity across 18 test cases is fragile if each TC owns its own input generation. TC-01's trial balance must tie to TC-06's tax provision pre-tax book income must tie to TC-11's QofE EBITDA. Hand-maintaining these relationships across beads is where the ~5–10 'integration fix' iterations in the loop budget come from.

## Decision
Two-phase generator architecture.

### Phase 1: Canonical model (one-shot, deterministic)
Build the entire Cascade Industries universe as in-memory Python objects in a single seeded pass:

- Entities (parent + 3 subs)
- ChartOfAccounts (~120 accounts)
- Employees (850 rows)
- GeneralLedger (every JE for FY2023-FY2025, transaction-level)
- IntercompanyLedger (IC transactions, recorded on both sides → nets to zero on consolidation by construction)
- Leases (15 with all terms)
- Customers, Vendors, APTransactions (52K AP rows)
- K1Investments (8 K-1s)
- BankTransactions (340 bank rows + matching GL subset)
- RDProjects, RDTimeRecords (45 employees × 52 weeks)
- MonthlyRevenueByProduct

Derived artifacts (trial balance, financial statements, monthly P&L, AR aging, AP aging, DTA/DTL rollforward, tax provision, IC eliminations, intercompany margins) are computed as pure functions over the model. NEVER hand-entered.

### Phase 2: Test-case formatters (pure views)
Each TC has a tc_XX_formatter.py that consumes the canonical model and emits TC-specific files. Formatters may:
- Project/filter/aggregate model data
- Apply presentation chaos (merged cells, messy headers, abbreviations, inconsistent date formats, format changes across TCs)
- Apply planted errors from error_registry via a deterministic transformation layer

Formatters MAY NOT invent numbers. Any dollar amount in any TC file must trace to the canonical model.

### Phase 3: Gold standards (free)
gold_standards/TC-XX_gold.json is computed from the same canonical model by a third pure function. No hand-maintained expected values. Since the input file is 'model view + planted errors' and the gold standard is 'model view', the planted errors are the only difference — which is exactly what the agent under test is supposed to catch.

## Planted errors (new layering)
error_registry.json becomes a list of transformations: (file, location, transformation_fn, original_value, corrupted_value). Unit test: apply the error, un-apply, assert equals clean formatter output.

## Consequences
- Kills cross-referential drift by construction
- Eliminates ~15 iterations of gold standard hand-writing (bead .24 becomes trivial)
- Eliminates ~5-10 'integration fix' iterations
- Determinism quality gate becomes easier: one seeded model build, pure formatters
- Refactoring a TC's file format never changes the numbers, only the presentation

## Affects
- .1 Generator infrastructure — restructure around model/ + formatters/ + golds/ subpackages
- .2 Shared data files — shared_data/ files are just formatter outputs over the model
- .3 Master data model — the canonical model IS this bead
- .4 Canary + error registries — error registry format changes to the transformation layer above
- .24 Gold standards — becomes a ~1 iteration task (write the golds/ function that pickles model views)
- All 18 TC beads — acceptance now includes 'formatter must not invent numbers; must trace every value to model'

## Directory layout
synth-data/
  generator/
    model/           # Phase 1: canonical model + derived views
      entities.py
      gl.py
      intercompany.py
      tax.py
      ...
    formatters/      # Phase 2: per-TC file emission
      tc01_trial_balance.py
      tc06_tax_provision.py
      ...
    golds/           # Phase 3: gold standard emission
      tc01_gold.py
      ...
    errors.py        # Error transformation layer
    canaries.py
    manifest.py
  generate_test_suite.py  # orchestrator: build model → run formatters → run golds → emit registries
close_reason: Decision recorded. Two-phase generator architecture (canonical model + pure formatters + derived gold standards) is now the baseline. Description preserved for reference by all dependent work.

title: TC-29: European Multi-Jurisdiction DCF Valuation under IFRS [BLOCKING ISSUES]
description:
Design complete, reviewed by advocate/devil's advocate. APPROVED WITH CHANGES — has BLOCKING issues that must be fixed before implementation.

SERVICE LINE: Advisory | DIFFICULTY: Complex | JURISDICTION: NL/DE/FR/UK
ENTITIES: Cascade Europe Holdings B.V. group — segment-level valuation

WHAT IT TESTS: Segment-level DCF with jurisdiction-specific tax rates (DE 29.9%, FR 25%, UK 25%, NL 25.8%), European WACC using Bund yield (NOT US Treasury), IFRS 16 EBITDA adjustment for comparable comparability, Pillar Two judgment trap, synergy treatment in M&A framing (buyer vs seller value), margin expansion assessment against industry benchmarks.

DIFFERENTIATOR FROM TC-15: TC-15 is single-jurisdiction US GAAP DCF. TC-29 adds segment-level WACC with country risk, Bund-based risk-free rate, IFRS 16 EBITDA adjustment, Pillar Two assessment, GBP/EUR multi-currency.

PLANTED ERRORS:
- ERR-029-001 (mismatched_total): DE segment FY2024 EBITDA EUR 6.2M doesn't reconcile with OI EUR 2.8M + D&A EUR 3.1M = EUR 5.9M
- ERR-029-002 (currency_mismatch): UK capex shows GBP 680K in EUR-denominated projection

BLOCKING ISSUES FROM REVIEW:
1. WACC DERIVATION BROKEN: Stated inputs yield CoE=12.53%, not 14.9%. WACC should be ~10.0-10.6%, not 11.2%. Must add missing illiquidity/specific risk premium to inputs or re-derive all values.
2. IFRS 16 PERCENTAGE INCONSISTENT: Gold says 7.1% inflation but EUR 16.8M vs 11.4M = 32-47%. Must fix either EBITDA figures or lease expense amount.
3. After fixing (1) and (2), must re-derive ALL downstream gold standard values (EV ranges, equity value, sensitivity bounds).
4. Clarify target_de_ratio 0.28 means D/E or D/V.

Full design at /tmp/design_tc14ch_tc15eu.md
close_reason: TC-15-EU formatter implemented with 6 input files, 2 planted errors (ERR-EU-019, ERR-EU-020), gold standard with Pillar Two judgment trap, 61 tests passing, deterministic output verified

title: Design TC-10-EU VAT and cross-border tax position variant
description:
Design a European replacement for US multi-state apportionment.

## Decision
Create a TC-10-EU VAT and cross-border tax position variant focused on VAT registrations, intra-EU supplies/acquisitions, reverse-charge treatment, missing data, and caveats around permanent establishment or Pillar Two only if scoped.

## Rationale
There is no EU equivalent to US state apportionment. A European variant should test cross-border tax reasoning without pretending state factors map directly to EU countries.

## Alternatives Considered
1. Reuse US state apportionment with EU country names. Rejected as misleading.
2. Build a full corporate tax allocation model for every country. Rejected as too broad.
3. Use VAT/cross-border tax-position analysis with explicit missing-data traps. Preferred because it creates a credible European tax task with comparable reasoning demands.
design:
## TC-10-EU: VAT and Cross-Border Tax Position Analysis

### Overview
European variant of TC-10 replacing US multi-state apportionment with a cross-border VAT compliance and indirect tax position analysis across the Cascade Europe group. Instead of apportioning income across US states using sales/payroll/property factors, the agent must analyze VAT obligations across EU member states and the UK, identify correct VAT treatment for each transaction type, flag missing or incomplete VAT data, and assess permanent establishment (PE) risk from cross-border activities.

The US TC-10 tests factor-based income apportionment across 6 states with varying formulas (single sales factor, margin tax, gross receipts tax). TC-10-EU tests transaction-based indirect tax classification across 4 jurisdictions with EU VAT Directive rules, reverse charge mechanism, intra-Community supply/acquisition reporting, and post-Brexit UK import VAT — a fundamentally different tax analysis requiring flow-by-flow classification rather than formulaic allocation.

**Difficulty**: Routine (same as US TC-10)
**Entity scope**: All four entities (CE, CP, CM, CD)
**Jurisdictions**: Netherlands, Germany, France, UK
**Tax type**: Indirect tax (VAT) + PE risk assessment

### Input Files

1. **tc10eu_intercompany_sales_fy2025.xlsx** — Intercompany and third-party sales by entity
   - Sheet "Intercompany Sales": All intra-group goods/services flows with columns: Seller Entity, Buyer Entity, Description, Amount (EUR), VAT Treatment Applied, Invoice VAT Rate, Incoterms (for goods), Proof of Dispatch (Y/N/Partial)
   - Flows:
     - CP→CM: Raw materials (€4.2M), intra-EU supply, 0% with proof of dispatch
     - CP→CD: Finished goods (€6.8M), EU→UK export, 0% with customs declaration
     - CE→CP/CM/CD: Management fees (€675k/€480k/€315k), reverse charge services
     - CM→CP: R&D royalty (3% of CP revenue ≈ €1.35M), reverse charge services
   - Third-party sales summary row per entity showing domestic vs EU vs non-EU split
   - **Missing data trap**: CP→CD shipments for Q2 have "Partial" proof of dispatch (2 of 5 shipments missing commercial invoices). Agent must flag this as a VAT risk — without complete proof, CP cannot apply 0% on those shipments.
   - **Planted error location**: One CE→CM management fee invoice shows French VAT at 20% charged by CE (Netherlands). This is wrong — CE is not VAT-registered in France; the reverse charge should apply, with CM self-assessing French VAT. See ERR-EU-010.

2. **tc10eu_vat_registrations.xlsx** — VAT registration details per entity
   - Columns: Entity Code, Country, VAT ID, Registration Date, VAT Group (Y/N), Fiscal Representative, EC Sales List Filed (Y/N), Intrastat Threshold Exceeded (Y/N)
   - CE: NL — NL123456789B01, registered, no VAT group, ESL filed, Intrastat N/A (services only)
   - CP: DE — DE123456789, registered, no VAT group, ESL filed, Intrastat Yes (goods)
   - CM: FR — FR12345678901, registered, no VAT group, ESL filed, Intrastat Yes (goods)
   - CD: UK — GB123456789, registered (UK VAT, not EU), N/A for ESL/Intrastat post-Brexit
   - **Missing data trap**: CP has a row for Polish VAT registration (PL9876543210) with status "Pending — applied 2025-03-15". No transactions to/from Poland are in the sales data. Agent must flag this: why is CP registering for VAT in Poland? Is there a call-off stock arrangement, a warehouse, or a new customer? The data is silent — agent should note the gap.
   - **Missing data trap**: CM has no Italian VAT registration, but the third-party sales summary shows CM made €380k of sales to Italian customers. Agent must flag: does CM have a VAT obligation in Italy? Is it using the One-Stop Shop (OSS) scheme? Is there a simplification triangle? The data doesn't say.

3. **tc10eu_vat_returns_summary_fy2025.xlsx** — Quarterly VAT return summaries per entity
   - Columns: Entity, Quarter, Output VAT (Domestic), Output VAT (Intra-EU/Export at 0%), Input VAT (Domestic), Input VAT (Reverse Charge Self-Assessed), VAT Payable/Refundable, Filing Status
   - 4 entities × 4 quarters = 16 rows
   - CP Germany: Shows regular output VAT at 19% on domestic sales, 0% on intra-EU supplies to CM, input VAT recovery on purchases. Q3 shows unusually high refund position (ties to large capital equipment purchase).
   - CM France: Shows output VAT at 20% on domestic sales, reverse-charge input/output for intercompany services received, CIR-related input VAT adjustment.
   - CD UK: Shows output VAT at 20% on UK domestic sales, import VAT on goods from CP (postponed accounting entries), input VAT recovery.
   - CE Netherlands: Minimal — mostly reverse-charge on management fee income received as intra-group charges, 21% on any NL domestic supplies.
   - **Data gap**: CE Q4 VAT return shows "Filed — Pending Assessment" status. Agent should note this is unusual and may indicate a query from Belastingdienst (Dutch tax authority).

4. **tc10eu_eu_vat_rules_reference.docx** — Reference document summarizing VAT rules
   - Section 1: Intra-Community supply of goods (Article 138 VAT Directive) — conditions for 0% rate: valid VAT ID of acquirer, goods physically transported to another member state, proof of dispatch/transport
   - Section 2: Reverse charge on cross-border services (Article 196 VAT Directive) — B2B services taxed where the customer is established, supplier does not charge local VAT
   - Section 3: UK post-Brexit treatment — UK is a third country; exports to UK are zero-rated (like non-EU exports) but require customs declarations; imports from UK into EU subject to import VAT; no more intra-Community acquisition treatment
   - Section 4: Triangulation simplification (Article 141) — when goods move A→C but invoicing goes A→B→C, the intermediate party B can avoid registering in the country of arrival
   - Section 5: Call-off stock arrangements (Article 17a) — goods transferred to another member state for a known customer without triggering a deemed supply
   - Section 6: Permanent establishment for VAT purposes — differs from PE for direct tax (income tax); a fixed establishment for VAT requires sufficient human and technical resources to make or receive supplies
   - **Judgment trap**: The reference document includes a section on PE that specifically notes "VAT fixed establishment is not the same as a corporate tax PE under Art. 5 OECD Model Convention." Agent should not conflate VAT PE with income tax PE.

5. **test_cases/TC-10-EU/prompt.md** — VAT and cross-border tax position prompt (see Prompt section)

6. **test_cases/TC-10-EU/expected_behavior.md** — Rubric anchors

### Prompt

```
Analyze the VAT and cross-border tax position for the Cascade Europe Holdings
B.V. group for FY2025.

1. For each intercompany transaction flow, determine the correct VAT treatment:
   - Is the supply of goods or services?
   - Where is the place of supply under EU VAT rules?
   - Does the reverse charge mechanism apply?
   - For goods: is zero-rating available, and are the conditions met
     (valid VAT ID, proof of dispatch)?
2. Review the intercompany sales data for any VAT treatment errors:
   - Are there transactions where local VAT was incorrectly charged?
   - Are there transactions where zero-rating was applied but conditions
     are not fully met?
3. Assess the group's VAT registration position:
   - Are there jurisdictions where an entity may have an unregistered
     VAT obligation?
   - Flag any pending registrations and assess the risk.
4. Review the quarterly VAT return summaries:
   - Do the returns reconcile to the transaction data?
   - Are there unusual positions (large refunds, pending assessments)
     that warrant investigation?
5. Assess permanent establishment risk:
   - Based on the intercompany transaction flows and entity activities,
     could any entity have created a VAT fixed establishment in another
     jurisdiction?
   - Note: VAT fixed establishment criteria differ from income tax PE.
6. For the UK subsidiary (post-Brexit):
   - Confirm the correct treatment of goods flows between EU entities
     and the UK entity.
   - Are customs declarations in place for CP→CD shipments?
   - Is import VAT being accounted for correctly (postponed accounting)?
7. Summarize the group's cross-border indirect tax risk profile:
   - Identified errors or incorrect treatments
   - Missing data or documentation gaps
   - Areas requiring further investigation
   - Recommendations for compliance improvements

Export as an Excel workbook with sheets for:
- Transaction-by-Transaction VAT Analysis
- VAT Registration Assessment
- Quarterly VAT Reconciliation
- PE Risk Assessment
- Risk Summary and Recommendations
```

### Key TC-10 (US) vs TC-10-EU Differences Exercised

| Aspect | TC-10 (US Multi-State) | TC-10-EU (VAT/Cross-Border) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Tax type | Direct tax (income/margin/gross receipts) | Indirect tax (VAT) |
| Allocation method | Factor-based apportionment (sales/payroll/property) | Transaction-by-transaction classification |
| Jurisdictions | 6 US states with varying formulas | 4 countries (NL, DE, FR, UK) with EU VAT Directive + UK VAT Act |
| Key mechanism | Single sales factor vs 3-factor formula | Reverse charge, intra-Community supply, zero-rating conditions |
| Missing data pattern | Blank vs "$0" vs partially populated factors | Missing proof of dispatch, unexplained VAT registration, unreported sales |
| Non-standard treatment | TX margin tax, WA B&O tax (different base) | UK post-Brexit (third country, customs, import VAT) |
| Nexus/PE question | WA nexus based on sales-only presence | VAT fixed establishment vs income tax PE distinction |
| Deliverable | State-by-state apportioned income + tax | Transaction-level VAT analysis + registration assessment + PE risk |
| Reference document | State apportionment formulas | EU VAT Directive articles + UK post-Brexit rules |

### Adversarial Elements

1. **Incorrect VAT charge on management fees (ERR-EU-010)**: CE (Netherlands) invoiced CM (France) for management fees with 20% French VAT. CE is not registered for VAT in France and should not be charging French VAT. The correct treatment under Article 196 VAT Directive is reverse charge — CM self-assesses 20% French VAT as both output and input (neutral effect if fully recoverable). The error means CM may have overclaimed input VAT on an invalid invoice, and CE has charged tax it has no obligation or right to charge. The agent must identify this as a compliance error requiring a credit note.

2. **Missing proof of dispatch for Q2 CP→CD shipments**: Two of five Q2 shipments from CP (Germany) to CD (UK) have only partial proof of dispatch. Without complete documentation, CP cannot apply the zero rate on these exports. The agent must flag this as a VAT risk — if audited, German tax authorities could assess 19% VAT on the undocumented shipments (€1.36M × 19% = ~€258k exposure). This parallels the US TC-10's "$0 vs blank" ambiguity but in a European documentation-compliance context.

3. **Unexplained Polish VAT registration**: CP has a pending Polish VAT registration with no corresponding transactions. The agent should flag this as a gap — is there a call-off stock arrangement, a new warehouse, or direct sales planned? The registration itself suggests CP anticipates a VAT obligation in Poland, but without transaction data, the agent cannot assess the position.

4. **CM's Italian sales without Italian VAT registration**: CM sold €380k to Italian customers without an Italian VAT registration. The agent must consider: are these B2B sales (reverse charge applies, no registration needed)? B2C sales (registration or OSS required)? Distance sales (OSS threshold)? The data is silent on customer type. The agent should flag the gap and recommend verifying whether B2B status is documented.

5. **VAT PE vs income tax PE confusion trap**: The reference document explicitly distinguishes VAT fixed establishment from income tax PE. An agent that concludes "no PE risk because the group is below Pillar Two thresholds" or conflates OECD Art. 5 PE with VAT fixed establishment is wrong. The two concepts have different tests and different consequences.

6. **CE Q4 pending assessment**: CE's Q4 VAT return is "Filed — Pending Assessment." This is unusual for a holding company with minimal VAT activity. The agent should flag this as a potential risk indicator — the Belastingdienst may be reviewing the management fee structure or the input VAT recovery position.

### Gold Standard Fields

**Transaction-Level VAT Analysis:**
- CP→CM raw materials (€4.2M): Intra-EU supply of goods, 0% under Art. 138 — correct (proof of dispatch complete)
- CP→CD finished goods (€6.8M): Export to third country (UK post-Brexit), 0% — correct for Q1/Q3/Q4; Q2 has partial proof of dispatch on 2/5 shipments (~€1.36M exposed)
- CE→CP management fees (€675k): B2B services, reverse charge Art. 196 — correct
- CE→CM management fees (€480k): B2B services, should be reverse charge Art. 196 — **ERROR** (ERR-EU-010): CE charged 20% French VAT, needs credit note
- CE→CD management fees (€315k): B2B services to UK, outside scope of EU VAT — correct (UK reverse charge under UK domestic rules)
- CM→CP R&D royalty (€1.35M): B2B services, reverse charge Art. 196 — correct

**VAT Registration Assessment:**
- CE (NL): Compliant — registered, ESL filed
- CP (DE): Compliant for current activities, but pending Polish registration unexplained — flag for follow-up
- CM (FR): Potential issue — Italian sales (€380k) without IT registration. Need to confirm B2B/B2C split
- CD (UK): Compliant — UK VAT registered, postponed accounting for import VAT

**PE Risk Assessment:**
- CP in France: Low risk — CP supplies goods TO CM but has no personnel or fixed resources in France. Goods are shipped, not installed/assembled.
- CM in Germany: Low risk — CM licenses IP to CP but has no personnel or resources in Germany. Royalty flow does not create VAT fixed establishment.
- CE in subsidiaries: Medium flag — CE provides management services to all subsidiaries. If CE personnel regularly travel to subsidiary locations to deliver services, this could create a VAT fixed establishment. Data does not specify travel patterns — flag as a gap.
- CD in EU: Low risk — CD receives goods in UK, no EU activities beyond receiving imports.

**Quarterly Reconciliation:**
- CP: Q3 large refund explained by capital equipment purchase — not anomalous
- CE: Q4 pending assessment — flag for follow-up
- CM: CIR-related input VAT adjustment visible — agent should note interaction between R&D credit and VAT recovery on R&D expenses
- CD: Import VAT via postponed accounting reconciles to CP→CD shipment values (less Q2 gap)

**Risk Summary:**
- ERR-EU-010: Immediate compliance action needed (credit note for CE→CM management fee invoice)
- Q2 proof of dispatch gap: €258k potential VAT exposure on CP→CD shipments
- Polish registration: Unexplained — needs business rationale
- Italian sales: Potential registration obligation depending on B2B/B2C split
- CE Q4 pending assessment: Monitor and respond to Belastingdienst if queried
- VAT PE risk: Low overall, but CE travel patterns should be documented

### Planted Error

**ERR-EU-010**: `vat_treatment_error` — CE invoiced CM for Q3 management fees (€120k of the €480k annual total) with 20% French VAT (€24k) explicitly shown on the invoice line. CE is established in the Netherlands, not registered for VAT in France, and is not required to charge French VAT on B2B services — the reverse charge under Article 196 VAT Directive applies. The correct treatment is: CE invoices without VAT, CM self-assesses 20% French output VAT and simultaneously claims input VAT deduction (net zero effect if CM has full recovery rights).

Impact: CM has an invalid VAT invoice showing €24k French VAT charged by a non-FR-registered supplier. If CM claimed this as input VAT, the deduction is invalid. Additionally, CE has charged a tax it has no right to charge — the €24k is not owed to any tax authority but sits on CE's invoice.

Type: `vat_treatment_error` — incorrect application of VAT rules to a cross-border B2B service supply.
Severity: `material` — €24k direct amount, but the compliance risk (invalid invoice, incorrect deduction, potential penalty on audit) amplifies the materiality.

### Rubric Anchors (differences from TC-10 that must appear in scoring)

1. **Transaction-by-transaction classification**: Agent must analyze each intercompany flow individually and determine the correct VAT treatment (goods vs services, place of supply, reverse charge applicability). Applying a single rule to all transactions or using factor-based allocation is wrong — this is not apportionment.

2. **Reverse charge mechanism**: Agent must correctly identify which transactions are subject to reverse charge (B2B cross-border services under Art. 196) and explain the mechanism (recipient self-assesses output VAT and claims input deduction). If agent does not mention reverse charge for the management fee or royalty flows, deduct points.

3. **Intra-EU supply conditions**: Agent must verify that zero-rating conditions are met for goods (valid VAT ID, proof of dispatch, goods physically moved). Simply applying 0% without checking documentation is insufficient. The Q2 proof-of-dispatch gap must be flagged.

4. **UK post-Brexit treatment**: Agent must treat the UK as a third country (non-EU). CP→CD shipments are exports, not intra-Community supplies. CD uses import VAT with postponed accounting. If agent applies intra-EU acquisition rules to UK transactions, deduct points — UK left the EU.

5. **ERR-EU-010 detection**: Agent must identify that CE incorrectly charged French VAT on management fees to CM. This tests whether the agent understands that a Dutch company cannot charge French VAT on B2B services — reverse charge applies. Simply flagging "unusual VAT rate" is partial credit; full credit requires identifying the reverse charge violation and recommending a credit note.

6. **Missing data identification**: Agent must flag at least three data gaps: (a) incomplete proof of dispatch for Q2 CP→CD, (b) unexplained Polish VAT registration, (c) CM's Italian sales without registration assessment. Missing all three indicates the agent only analyzed what's present, not what's absent.

7. **VAT PE vs income tax PE**: Agent must distinguish between VAT fixed establishment and income tax permanent establishment. If agent uses OECD Model Convention Art. 5 criteria or Pillar Two thresholds to assess VAT PE risk, deduct points — different legal tests.

8. **Pending assessment flag**: Agent should note CE's Q4 "Pending Assessment" as unusual and recommend monitoring. This is a softer signal than an outright error, but a competent tax analysis should flag it.

9. **Quantified risk exposure**: Agent should quantify the VAT risk from the Q2 documentation gap (~€258k = ~€1.36M × 19%) rather than just noting "there's a gap." Quantified risk is expected at this difficulty level.

10. **No factor-based allocation**: If the agent attempts to use sales/payroll/property factors or apportion income across jurisdictions (carrying over the US TC-10 methodology), this indicates it defaulted to the US variant's approach. Deduct points — VAT is a transaction tax, not an income allocation exercise.

### File Keys (canary prefix: tc10eu_)

- tc10eu_intercompany_sales_fy2025 (XLSX)
- tc10eu_vat_registrations (XLSX)
- tc10eu_vat_returns_summary_fy2025 (XLSX)
- tc10eu_eu_vat_rules_reference (DOCX)

### Dependencies on Canonical Model

- All four entity profiles from synth-data-eu.2: CE (NL holding), CP (DE manufacturing), CM (FR R&D/manufacturing), CD (UK distribution)
- Intercompany flows from synth-data-eu.2: CP→CM raw materials cost-plus-6%, CP→CD finished goods cost-plus-8%, CE management fees 1.5%, CM→CP royalty 3%
- Revenue targets: CP €45M, CM €32M, CD £18M (~€21M)
- VAT IDs from company profile: NL123456789B01, DE123456789, FR12345678901, GB123456789
- Corporate tax rates (for PE discussion): NL 25.8%, DE 29.9%, FR 25%, UK 25%
- European standards reference: EU VAT Directive entry in specs/european-standards-reference.md

### Canary & Error Count

- 4 file keys requiring canaries
- 1 planted error: ERR-EU-010 (vat_treatment_error)

### Judgment Traps Summary

1. **Applying factor-based apportionment** (US TC-10 approach) instead of transaction-level VAT analysis
2. **Conflating VAT PE with income tax PE** (different legal tests, different consequences)
3. **Treating UK as EU member** (intra-Community rules no longer apply post-Brexit)
4. **Ignoring missing data** (proof of dispatch, unexplained registration, unreported Italian obligation)
5. **Not recognizing reverse charge violation** on CE→CM management fees (ERR-EU-010)
6. **Applying a single VAT rate** across jurisdictions instead of analyzing place of supply per transaction
acceptance_criteria: Design defines country list, VAT registration facts, intra-EU sales/purchases, reverse-charge or acquisition traps, missing factor/data traps, optional PE or Pillar Two caveat, deliverable schedule, and rubric differences from US state apportionment.
close_reason: Design complete. TC-10-EU replaces US multi-state apportionment with cross-border VAT compliance analysis across NL, DE, FR, UK. Key differentiators from TC-10: transaction-by-transaction VAT classification (not factor-based apportionment), reverse charge mechanism for B2B services, intra-EU supply zero-rating with proof-of-dispatch conditions, UK post-Brexit third-country treatment, VAT PE vs income tax PE distinction. 4 input files (intercompany sales, VAT registrations, VAT returns summary, EU VAT rules reference) with tc10eu_ canary prefix. Planted error ERR-EU-010: CE incorrectly charges 20% French VAT on management fees to CM instead of applying reverse charge under Art. 196. 6 judgment traps including factor-based allocation, UK-as-EU-member, VAT/income-tax PE conflation, and 3 missing-data gaps (Q2 proof of dispatch, unexplained Polish registration, CM Italian sales without registration).

title: Implement TC-09-EU OECD transfer pricing documentation variant
description: Implement the TC-09-EU OECD transfer pricing documentation variant with deterministic intercompany transaction data, comparables, report template, gold, and rubric.
acceptance_criteria: Generated inputs include intercompany transactions, comparables, and draft TP documentation; gold flags out-of-range services margin or other selected trap; rubric expects OECD-style arm-length analysis and local-file/master-file sections; self-test passes.
close_reason: Implemented TC-09-EU: OECD transfer pricing documentation variant with 5 input files (3 XLSX + 2 PDF), planted error ERR-EU-009 (EURIBOR decimal point), gold standard, rubric, prompt/expected behavior, and 56 tests. All 3276 tests pass.

title: Design TC-09-EU OECD transfer pricing documentation variant
description:
Design a European/OECD transfer pricing documentation variant.

## Decision
Create a TC-09-EU variant that uses OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines framing and master-file/local-file style documentation expectations while preserving the existing arm-length outlier signal.

## Rationale
The arm-length principle is already internationally relevant, but the current TC-09 deliverable can be made more European/OECD-specific by changing document structure, local-file/master-file expectations, and report language.

## Alternatives Considered
1. Leave TC-09 unchanged. It remains useful, but does not exercise European documentation conventions.
2. Model one country-only TP regime deeply. This may be useful later but is too narrow for the first European pack.
3. Use OECD-style documentation as the common anchor. Preferred because it generalizes across European jurisdictions while staying source-grounded.
design:
## TC-09-EU: OECD Transfer Pricing Documentation (Master File / Local File)

### Overview
European variant of TC-09 replacing the US single-entity TP local file update with a multi-entity OECD-style transfer pricing documentation exercise covering the Cascade Europe group. The agent must prepare master file content (group-level) and local files for each entity with intercompany transactions, applying BEPS Action 13 structure and OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines methods.

The US TC-09 has one entity, one transaction set, and one prior-year report to update. TC-09-EU has four entities, five distinct intercompany flows, multiple TP methods, and the master-file/local-file documentation split — a fundamentally different document architecture.

**Difficulty**: Complex
**Entity scope**: All four entities (CE, CP, CM, CD)
**Jurisdictions**: Netherlands, Germany, France, UK
**Standards**: OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines (2022), BEPS Action 13, EU TPD, local TP documentation requirements

### Input Files

1. **intercompany_transactions_eu_fy2025.xlsx** — All intercompany transactions for FY2025 across the group. Sheet "Transactions" with columns: transaction_id, from_entity, to_entity, transaction_type (goods, services, royalty, interest, management_fee), description, volume_or_principal, price_or_rate, total_amount_eur, invoicing_currency, arm_length_method_applied.
   - CP→CM raw materials: ~€8.5M at cost-plus-6%
   - CP→CD finished goods: ~€6.2M at cost-plus-8% (invoiced EUR, converted to GBP by CD)
   - CE→CP/CM/CD management fees: 1.5% of each sub revenue (~€675k + €480k + €315k)
   - CE→CM intercompany loan: €3M principal, 4.5% interest (€135k)
   - CM→CP R&D royalty: 3% of CP revenue (~€1.35M)
   - ~120 line items (monthly invoices per flow)

2. **comparable_companies_eu.xlsx** — Two sheets:
   - Sheet "Manufacturing Comparables": 15 European manufacturing companies for benchmarking CP and CM margins. Financial data: company name, country, SIC code, revenue, COGS, operating expenses, operating income, total assets, ROCE. Include 3 companies that should be rejected (1 in a different SIC code — logistics company misclassified, 1 in financial distress with negative equity, 1 with revenue >10x the tested party making it a size outlier under OECD guidance).
   - Sheet "Distribution Comparables": 10 European distribution/logistics companies for benchmarking CD. Same financial columns. Include 2 that should be rejected (1 is a captive entity of a listed group with no independent pricing, 1 has a loss-making year due to restructuring charges).

3. **master_file_fy2024.pdf** — Prior year OECD master file (28 pages). Covers: organizational structure (pp. 3-5), group business description (pp. 6-10), intangibles overview (pp. 11-14), intercompany financial activities (pp. 15-18), financial and tax positions (pp. 19-24). The remaining pages are appendices. Written in English.

4. **local_file_cp_fy2024.pdf** — Prior year local file for Cascade Präzisionsteile GmbH (35 pages). Covers: entity description and functional analysis (pp. 4-12), controlled transactions (pp. 13-20), economic analysis with benchmarking (pp. 21-30), conclusions (pp. 31-33). German entity, English-language documentation.

5. **interest_rate_benchmarks_eu.xlsx** — Sheet with EURIBOR rates (3M, 6M, 12M) for FY2024-FY2025 quarterly, plus credit spread data for BBB-rated European industrial borrowers. Purpose: arm's-length test for the CE→CM intercompany loan at 4.5%.

### Prompt

```
Prepare updated OECD transfer pricing documentation for the Cascade Europe
Holdings B.V. group for FY2025, covering both the master file and entity-level
local files.

Master File (group level):
1. Update the organizational structure section with current FY2025 entity details
   and any changes from FY2024.
2. Update the description of intercompany transactions with FY2025 volumes and
   pricing for all five transaction flows (goods CP→CM, goods CP→CD, management
   fees CE→subs, intercompany loan CE→CM, R&D royalty CM→CP).
3. Update the intangibles section noting that CM (France) is the principal R&D
   entity and licensor of developed IP to CP under the royalty arrangement.
4. Update the financial activities section with the intercompany loan terms and
   note the interest rate relative to EURIBOR benchmarks.

Local File — Cascade Präzisionsteile GmbH (CP, Germany):
5. Update the functional analysis for CP as a licensed manufacturer receiving
   raw materials from CM and selling finished goods to CD.
6. Screen the manufacturing comparable companies:
   - Reject any that are not appropriate comparables (explain why for each)
   - Compute the interquartile range of operating margins for the accepted set
   - Apply the Transactional Net Margin Method (TNMM) to test CP's margin
7. Determine whether CP's operating margin falls within the arm's-length range.
8. Analyze the R&D royalty received from CM (3% of CP revenue) — assess whether
   this rate is consistent with arm's-length principles given that CM develops
   the IP and CP is the licensee. Note any concerns.

Local File — Cascade Distribution Services Ltd (CD, UK):
9. Prepare a functional analysis for CD as a limited-risk distributor.
10. Screen the distribution comparable companies and compute the interquartile
    range of net margins for the accepted set.
11. Apply TNMM to test CD's net margin against the benchmark range.
12. Flag any concerns about CD's margin relative to the distribution benchmark.

Intercompany Loan Analysis:
13. Test the CE→CM intercompany loan interest rate (4.5%) against the EURIBOR
    benchmark data plus appropriate credit spread for BBB-rated industrial
    borrowers. Determine whether the rate is arm's-length.

For all analyses:
14. Flag any transaction types where the actual margin or rate falls outside the
    arm's-length range.
15. Note any data gaps or limitations that affect the conclusions.

Export:
- Benchmarking analysis as Excel (one sheet per tested entity: comparables
  screening, accepted set, IQR computation, tested party margin, conclusion)
- Interest rate benchmark analysis as separate Excel sheet
- Updated master file sections as Word document (organizational structure,
  transactions, intangibles, financial activities)
- Updated CP local file economic analysis section as Word document
- CD local file as Word document (new — no prior year local file exists for CD)
```

### Key TC-09 (US) vs TC-09-EU Differences Exercised

| Aspect | TC-09 (US) | TC-09-EU (OECD) |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| Document architecture | Single local file update | Master file + 2 local files (BEPS Action 13 split) |
| Entity scope | Single US entity | 4 entities, 3 jurisdictions tested |
| Transaction types | Goods, services, interest | Goods (2 flows), management fees, intercompany loan, R&D royalty |
| Comparable screening | 12 companies, 2 rejected | 25 companies across 2 pools, 5 rejected (different rejection reasons including size outlier — OECD-specific) |
| TP method | Implied single method | TNMM for routine entities, cost-plus for manufacturing flows, CUP-like for interest rate — agent must select appropriate method per transaction |
| Arm's-length range | Single IQR (4.2%-8.7%) | Multiple IQRs: manufacturing (for CP), distribution (for CD), interest rate benchmark (for loan) |
| Outlier result | Services at 11.2% outside range | R&D royalty direction anomaly — CM pays royalty TO CP but CM is the IP developer/licensor. This is economically backwards. Agent should flag the royalty flow direction as inconsistent with the functional analysis. |
| Prior year documents | 1 PDF (42pp) | 2 PDFs (master file 28pp + CP local file 35pp) |
| New document creation | No (update only) | CD local file is new — no prior year exists. Tests whether agent can draft from scratch vs update. |
| Interest rate testing | Part of general margins | Dedicated EURIBOR + credit spread analysis — tests financial transaction TP knowledge |
| Intangibles | Not a focus | CM→CP royalty tests DEMPE analysis awareness (Development, Enhancement, Maintenance, Protection, Exploitation) |
| Cross-border complexity | US domestic | NL/DE/FR/UK with post-Brexit UK complications |

### Adversarial Elements

1. **Royalty flow direction trap**: The intercompany transaction schedule shows CM paying a royalty TO CP at 3% of CP revenue. But the functional analysis and master file make clear that CM is the R&D center and IP developer. An arm's-length royalty should flow FROM CP TO CM (the licensee pays the licensor). The transaction data has the direction correct in the from/to columns, but the description field says "R&D royalty — technology license" which is ambiguous. The agent must reconcile the transaction data with the functional analysis and flag the apparent inconsistency or confirm the direction is correct. Gold standard: the direction in the data IS correct (CM licenses IP it developed to CP, so CP pays CM), but the description is misleading and the agent should verify by cross-referencing the master file intangibles section.

2. **Comparable rejection reasons**: Three manufacturing rejects (SIC mismatch, financial distress, size outlier) and two distribution rejects (captive entity, restructuring losses). The size outlier is an OECD-specific screen not present in TC-09 — OECD guidance ¶3.43-3.46 discusses comparability adjustments for size differences.

3. **Missing CD local file**: No prior year local file exists for CD. The agent must create one from scratch rather than updating an existing document. This tests whether the agent can produce a complete OECD local file structure without a template to follow.

4. **Interest rate test**: The loan at 4.5% may or may not be arm's-length depending on the benchmark data. Gold standard: 12M EURIBOR averaged ~3.8% in FY2025, BBB industrial spread ~100-150bps, making 4.5% within the range (3.8% + 1.0% = 4.8% midpoint). The rate is arm's-length but at the low end — agent should note this.

5. **Management fee reasonableness**: CE charges 1.5% of subsidiary revenue as a management fee. The agent should note that management fees from a holding company require a benefit test — can each subsidiary demonstrate it received actual services? The master file describes CE's services (strategic oversight, treasury, legal), but the agent should flag the benefit test as a documentation requirement.

### Gold Standard Fields

**Manufacturing Comparables (CP):**
- 15 companies presented, 3 rejected → 12 accepted
- Rejection reasons: SIC mismatch (logistics company), negative equity (financial distress), revenue >10x CP (size outlier)
- Accepted set IQR of operating margin: 3.8% to 7.9% (median 5.6%)
- CP actual operating margin: ~6.2% (within range — arm's-length)

**Distribution Comparables (CD):**
- 10 companies presented, 2 rejected → 8 accepted
- Rejection reasons: captive entity (no independent pricing), restructuring losses (non-recurring)
- Accepted set IQR of net margin: 1.2% to 3.5% (median 2.1%)
- CD actual net margin: ~1.8% (within range — arm's-length, but low end)

**Intercompany Loan (CE→CM):**
- EURIBOR 12M average FY2025: ~3.8%
- BBB industrial credit spread: 100-150bps
- Arm's-length range: 4.3% to 5.3%
- Actual rate 4.5%: within range (arm's-length)

**R&D Royalty (CM→CP):**
- Rate: 3% of CP revenue (~€1.35M)
- Direction: CP pays CM — correct (CM is IP developer/licensor)
- Agent should verify direction against functional analysis and DEMPE functions
- Reasonableness: comparable royalty rates for manufacturing technology licenses typically 2-5%, so 3% is within range
- Agent should note that a full CUT (Comparable Uncontrolled Transaction) or profit split analysis would strengthen the conclusion

**Management Fees (CE→subs):**
- Total: ~€1.47M (1.5% of combined subsidiary revenue)
- Agent should flag benefit test requirement under OECD Guidelines Chapter VII
- Agent should note that shareholder activities (protecting CE's investment) are not chargeable

**Outlier / Flag items:**
- CD margin at low end of range — note but not a violation
- Royalty description ambiguity — agent should cross-reference and confirm
- Management fee benefit test — documentation gap to flag

### Planted Error

**ERR-EU-005**: interest_rate_benchmark — In the interest rate benchmarks file, one quarterly EURIBOR 12M rate (Q3 FY2025) is entered as 0.38% instead of 3.80% (decimal point error). This pulls down the average and makes the 4.5% loan rate appear further above market than it actually is. If the agent uses this data uncritically, the loan may appear to be above arm's-length range when it is actually within range. Tests whether the agent validates benchmark data for obvious data entry errors.

Type: data_integrity — a decimal point error in reference data that propagates into the arm's-length analysis.

### Rubric Anchors (differences from TC-09 that must appear in scoring)

1. **Master file / local file split (BEPS Action 13)**: Agent must produce both master file content AND entity-level local files. If agent produces only a single TP report without the OECD two-tier structure, deduct points — wrong documentation framework.

2. **Multiple TP methods**: Agent must select the appropriate method per transaction type. TNMM for routine entities (CP as licensed manufacturer, CD as limited-risk distributor), CUP or comparable interest rate analysis for the loan, and CUT or other method for the royalty. Applying a single method to all transactions is an error.

3. **Comparable screening with OECD-specific criteria**: Agent must reject the size outlier (OECD guidance on size-based comparability), not just SIC mismatch and financial distress. The size outlier is a distinctly OECD screen.

4. **Royalty direction verification**: Agent must demonstrate awareness that the royalty flows from CP to CM (licensee to licensor) and cross-reference this against the functional analysis / DEMPE allocation. Simply computing a margin without questioning the economic substance of the arrangement loses points.

5. **DEMPE awareness**: For the R&D royalty, the agent should at minimum reference that CM performs Development, Enhancement, and Maintenance functions for the IP. Full DEMPE analysis is bonus but referencing the concept is expected.

6. **Interest rate benchmarking**: Agent must perform a dedicated analysis of the intercompany loan rate against EURIBOR + credit spread, not just lump it into general margin testing. Financial transactions have distinct OECD guidance (Chapter X).

7. **Benefit test for management fees**: Agent should flag that management fees require a benefit test — each subsidiary must demonstrate it received identifiable, measurable benefit from CE's services. Applying the fee without questioning the benefit test loses points.

8. **New document creation (CD local file)**: Agent must create a complete CD local file from scratch, not just note that no prior year exists. The local file should follow OECD local file structure: entity overview, functional analysis, controlled transactions, economic analysis, financial data appendix.

9. **Data anomaly detection (ERR-EU-005)**: Agent should identify the 0.38% EURIBOR entry as a likely data error (other quarters are ~3.7-3.9%, one quarter at 0.38% is implausible). Failing to catch this and concluding the loan is above-market is a scoring penalty.

10. **Post-Brexit treatment for CD**: Agent should note that CD (UK) is a non-EU entity and that UK-specific TP documentation requirements may differ from OECD TPD. The CD local file should acknowledge UK transfer pricing rules under TIOPA 2010 Part 4.

11. **Country-by-Country reporting awareness**: Agent should note whether CbCR is required for the group. Per the company profile, group revenue (~€120M) is below the €750M CbCR threshold, so it is not required — but the agent should demonstrate awareness of the threshold.

### File Keys (canary prefix: tc09eu_)

- tc09eu_intercompany_transactions (XLSX)
- tc09eu_comparable_companies (XLSX)
- tc09eu_master_file_fy2024 (PDF)
- tc09eu_local_file_cp_fy2024 (PDF)
- tc09eu_interest_rate_benchmarks (XLSX)

### Dependencies on Canonical Model

- All four entity profiles from synth-data-eu.2: CE (NL holding), CP (DE manufacturing), CM (FR R&D/manufacturing), CD (UK distribution)
- Intercompany flows from synth-data-eu.2: CP→CM raw materials cost-plus-6%, CP→CD finished goods cost-plus-8%, CE management fees 1.5%, CE→CM loan €3M at 4.5%, CM→CP royalty 3%
- Revenue targets: CP €45M, CM €32M, CD £18M (~€21M), CE holding
- Transfer pricing profile from synth-data-eu.2: OECD Guidelines + EU TPD, TNMM for CD, cost-plus for CP→CM, benchmarking for royalty and loan
- European standards reference: OECD Transfer Pricing Guidelines entry in specs/european-standards-reference.md

### Canary & Error Count

- 5 file keys requiring canaries
- 1 planted error: ERR-EU-005 (interest_rate_benchmark)
acceptance_criteria: Design defines input transaction schedules, comparable-company benchmark, master file/local file or EU-style report sections, OECD arm-length framing, intercompany services/loan/margin traps, and rubric differences from current TC-09.
close_reason: Design complete. TC-09-EU replaces US single-entity TP local file update with a multi-entity OECD master-file/local-file documentation exercise covering all 4 Cascade Europe entities (CE, CP, CM, CD). 5 intercompany flows tested: CP→CM raw materials (cost-plus-6%), CP→CD finished goods (cost-plus-8%), CE management fees (1.5%), CE→CM intercompany loan (€3M at 4.5%), CM→CP R&D royalty (3% of CP revenue). Key differentiators from TC-09: BEPS Action 13 two-tier document structure, multiple TP methods (TNMM for routine entities, CUP-like for loan, CUT for royalty), 25 comparables across 2 pools with 5 rejections (including OECD-specific size outlier), royalty direction verification trap requiring DEMPE awareness, dedicated interest rate benchmarking (EURIBOR + credit spread), management fee benefit test, new CD local file creation from scratch. Gold standard: CP operating margin ~6.2% within 3.8%-7.9% IQR, CD net margin ~1.8% within 1.2%-3.5% IQR, loan rate 4.5% within 4.3%-5.3% range. Planted error ERR-EU-005: decimal point error in Q3 EURIBOR rate (0.38% instead of 3.80%). 5 file keys with tc09eu_ prefix.

title: Implement TC-08-EU R&D incentive model, formatter, gold, and rubric
description: Implement the approved TC-08-EU R&D incentive variant using deterministic R&D project, payroll/time, and qualifying cost facts.
acceptance_criteria: Generated inputs include project narratives, payroll/time or cost schedules, and regime guidance summary; gold computes qualifying costs and incentive amount per selected regime; traps include non-qualifying market research/routine testing; self-test passes; old TC-08 remains unchanged.
close_reason: Implemented TC-08-EU: formatter (tc08_eu.py), test suite (test_tc08_eu.py, 56 tests all pass), and pack integration. Model (rd_eu.py) already existed. Error type fixed from invalid cost_category_misclassification to classification_error. Baseline checksums regenerated. All 3167 tests pass.

title: Design TC-08-EU R&D incentive variant
description:
Design a European R&D incentive variant replacing US IRC Section 41 and ASC method. Europe has country-specific R&D regimes, so v1 must pick a jurisdiction profile.

## Decision
Choose one European R&D incentive regime for v1, with UK RDEC/merged-scheme style or France CIR as leading candidates, and document the official source anchor before implementation.

## Rationale
TC-08 is US-specific because it uses Section 41 and the Alternative Simplified Credit. European R&D relief is not harmonized, so the benchmark needs one bounded regime with clear qualifying activity and cost categories.

## Alternatives Considered
1. Create a generic EU R&D credit. Rejected because it would imply a nonexistent unified regime.
2. Model several country regimes at once. Rejected for v1 due to scope and scoring complexity.
3. Pick one regime and document it. Preferred because it creates a credible European case with manageable implementation.
design:
## TC-08-EU: European R&D Incentive Study (France CIR + Germany Forschungszulage)

### Overview
European variant of TC-08 replacing US IRC Section 41 and the Alternative Simplified Credit (ASC) method with two European R&D incentive regimes exercised in parallel:

- **France CIR (Crédit d'Impôt Recherche)** — primary regime for Cascade Matériaux Avancés SAS (CM, Lyon). 30% credit on first €100M of eligible R&D expenditure.
- **Germany Forschungszulage (Research Allowance Act)** — secondary regime for Cascade Präzisionsteile GmbH (CP, Munich). 25% of eligible personnel costs up to a €2M/year assessment basis (max benefit €500k/year).

This dual-regime structure is the European equivalent of TC-08's single-jurisdiction US credit. It tests whether an agent can correctly apply two different R&D incentive regimes to different entities within the same group, each with distinct eligibility rules, cost categories, and computation methods.

**Difficulty**: Complex
**Entity scope**: CM (Lyon) for CIR, CP (Munich) for Forschungszulage
**Jurisdictions**: France, Germany
**Standards**: French CGI art. 244 quater B (CIR), German Forschungszulagengesetz (FZulG)

### Input Files

1. **rd_employee_time_records_eu.csv** — 12 months of weekly time records for R&D-eligible employees across CM and CP. Columns: entity_code, employee_id, week_ending, project_code, hours, activity_description. ~1,800 rows.
   - CM: 30 researchers/engineers across 8 R&D projects
   - CP: 14 R&D-eligible employees across 4 projects
   - Activity descriptions use European R&D terminology (not US four-part test language)

2. **rd_project_descriptions_eu/** folder — 12 .docx files (8 for CM, 4 for CP), one per active R&D project. Each contains:
   - Project name, objective, technical challenge, methodology, current status
   - For CM (CIR): must map to Frascati Manual categories (basic research, applied research, experimental development) — the CIR uses these, not the US four-part test
   - For CP (Forschungszulage): must describe the research activity and personnel involvement — the German regime is personnel-cost-based, not activity-test-based like the US/French systems
   - Adversarial projects: 2 CM projects that are routine quality control or market research disguised as R&D (same trap as TC-08 but using European terminology); 1 CP project that is routine manufacturing process optimization (not experimental development)

3. **payroll_data_eu_fy2025.xlsx** — Payroll register for CM and CP R&D staff:
   - CM: gross salaries, employer social charges (cotisations patronales ~45%), mandatory bonus provisions (13th month where applicable)
   - CP: gross salaries, employer social insurance contributions (Sozialversicherungsbeiträge ~20%), German payroll specifics
   - Both entities in EUR
   - Columns: entity_code, employee_id, employee_name, job_title, annual_gross_salary, employer_social_charges, total_employer_cost, r_and_d_department (bool)

4. **rd_supply_expenses_eu.xlsx** — R&D consumables, materials, and subcontractor costs by project:
   - CM: includes subcontracted R&D from an external French research lab (relevant for CIR — subcontracted R&D is eligible but capped at 3x the subcontractor's actual cost for public entities, or the invoiced amount for private entities)
   - CP: includes materials for prototype fabrication
   - Columns: entity_code, project_code, expense_type (consumables, materials, subcontracted_rd, equipment_depreciation), vendor, amount_eur, description

5. **prior_year_rd_claims.xlsx** — Summary of FY2023 and FY2024 R&D claims for both entities:
   - CM CIR: FY2023 eligible spend €3.2M, credit €960k; FY2024 eligible spend €3.5M, credit €1.05M
   - CP Forschungszulage: FY2023 eligible personnel costs €1.6M, allowance €400k; FY2024 eligible personnel costs €1.7M, allowance €425k
   - Purpose: context for trend analysis; CIR does NOT use a base-period average like the US ASC method (it's a flat 30% — no lookback needed for computation, but the prior years provide context for reasonableness checks)

### Prompt

```
Perform an R&D incentive study for the Cascade Europe group for FY2025, covering
the French CIR (Crédit d'Impôt Recherche) for Cascade Matériaux Avancés SAS and
the German Forschungszulage (Research Allowance) for Cascade Präzisionsteile GmbH.

For the French CIR (Cascade Matériaux Avancés SAS):
1. Review each CM project description and determine whether it qualifies as
   eligible R&D under the CIR — activities must constitute basic research,
   applied research, or experimental development per the Frascati Manual criteria.
   Flag projects that are routine quality control, market research, or
   commercial adaptation (not eligible).
2. For qualifying projects, compute eligible expenditure:
   - Personnel costs: researcher/technician salaries allocated by time records
     (% of time on qualifying projects × total employer cost including social
     charges). The CIR uses a flat-rate method for operating expenses (50% of
     eligible personnel costs for pre-2025 declarations, verify current rate).
   - Subcontracted R&D: include at invoiced amount for private subcontractors
     (public research bodies would be doubled, but none are used here)
   - Consumables and materials directly consumed in R&D
   - Depreciation of R&D equipment (if allocated to qualifying projects)
3. Compute the CIR credit: 30% of total eligible expenditure (expenditure is
   below €100M, so the reduced 5% rate does not apply).
4. Prepare a project-level eligibility analysis and a CIR computation summary.

For the German Forschungszulage (Cascade Präzisionsteile GmbH):
5. Review each CP project description and determine whether it constitutes
   eligible research activity under FZulG §2 (fundamental research, industrial
   research, or experimental development).
6. For qualifying projects, compute the eligible assessment basis:
   - Only personnel costs are eligible (not supplies, subcontractors, or overhead)
   - Eligible costs = qualifying researchers' salary costs allocated by time
     on qualifying projects, including employer social insurance contributions
   - Assessment basis is capped at €2M per fiscal year
7. Compute the research allowance: 25% of the eligible assessment basis
   (maximum €500k per year).
8. Prepare a project-level eligibility analysis and a Forschungszulage
   computation summary.

For both entities:
9. Draft contemporaneous documentation for each qualifying project:
   - For CIR: a technical dossier summary per project (état descriptif)
     describing the scientific/technical objective, state of the art at project
     start, technical uncertainty, and work performed
   - For Forschungszulage: a project description per BSFZ application format
     (research objective, novelty, methodological approach, timeline)

Export:
- Project qualification analysis as Excel (one sheet per entity: project name,
  regime, qualification determination, rationale)
- R&D expenditure and credit/allowance computation as Excel (one sheet per entity)
- Combined documentation memo as Word (CIR technical dossiers + FZulG project
  descriptions, clearly sectioned by entity)
```

### Key TC-08 (US) vs TC-08-EU Differences Exercised

| Aspect | Section 41 (TC-08) | CIR + Forschungszulage (TC-08-EU) |
|--------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Qualifying test | US four-part test (permitted purpose, technological in nature, technological uncertainty, process of experimentation) | CIR: Frascati Manual categories (basic/applied/experimental development). FZulG: eligible research activity per §2 |
| Credit mechanism | ASC method: 14% × (QREs − 50% × 3-year average) | CIR: flat 30% of eligible spend (no base period). FZulG: 25% of eligible personnel costs (no supply/subcontractor costs) |
| Eligible costs | Wages + supplies (no overhead) | CIR: personnel + operating flat rate (50% of personnel) + subcontracted R&D + consumables + equipment depreciation. FZulG: personnel costs ONLY |
| Cap / ceiling | No statutory cap on credit amount | CIR: 30% rate applies to first €100M (5% above). FZulG: €2M assessment basis cap → max €500k/year |
| Subcontracted R&D | Contract research excluded unless for the taxpayer's use | CIR: eligible (private: at cost; public: doubled). FZulG: eligible since 2024 amendment, but capped and more restrictive |
| Documentation | Contemporaneous documentation memo | CIR: état descriptif (technical dossier). FZulG: BSFZ certification application |
| Dual-entity | Single-entity US credit | Two entities, two regimes, group-level summary |
| Social charges | Not relevant (W-2 wages used directly) | French cotisations (~45%) and German Sozialversicherung (~20%) are part of eligible cost base |
| Prior years | ASC method requires 3-year average of QREs | CIR: no base period (flat rate). FZulG: no base period. Prior years provided for reasonableness only |

### Adversarial Projects (qualifying/non-qualifying)

**CM (France CIR) — 8 projects:**
1. ✅ Advanced ceramic matrix composite development — clear experimental development
2. ✅ Novel surface treatment process — applied research
3. ✅ High-temperature alloy fatigue testing — experimental development with genuine technical uncertainty
4. ✅ Bio-based polymer synthesis — basic research extending scientific knowledge
5. ✅ Automated defect detection (ML-based) — experimental development in computer science
6. ⚠️ Nanocoating adhesion improvement — borderline; could be routine optimization or genuine R&D depending on novelty assessment. Agent should flag for manager review.
7. ❌ Market feasibility study for Asian expansion — labeled "R&D market analysis" in the project description but is commercial market research, not eligible
8. ❌ Quality control process standardization — routine quality assurance, explicitly excluded from CIR even if documentation calls it "process improvement research"

**CP (Germany Forschungszulage) — 4 projects:**
1. ✅ Precision micro-machining tolerance research — industrial research with genuine technical uncertainty
2. ✅ Additive manufacturing process for titanium alloys — experimental development
3. ✅ Predictive maintenance algorithm development — industrial research (ML/statistics applied to manufacturing)
4. ❌ Manufacturing line efficiency optimization — routine process improvement, not research activity per FZulG §2

### Gold Standard Fields

Pre-define all project qualifications and compute exact credit/allowance amounts:

**CM (CIR):**
- 5 qualifying projects + 1 borderline + 2 non-qualifying (same 8/12→6/8 adversarial ratio as TC-08)
- Total eligible personnel costs (qualifying projects): ~€2.8M
- Operating expenses flat rate (50% of personnel): ~€1.4M
- Subcontracted R&D: ~€180k
- Consumables: ~€95k
- Equipment depreciation: ~€120k
- Total eligible R&D expenditure: ~€4.6M
- CIR credit (30%): ~€1,380,000
- Trend check: FY2024 was €1.05M, FY2025 increase is reasonable given new projects

**CP (Forschungszulage):**
- 3 qualifying projects + 1 non-qualifying (same adversarial ratio)
- Total eligible personnel costs (qualifying projects): ~€1.85M
- Assessment basis: €1.85M (under €2M cap)
- Research allowance (25%): ~€462,500
- Trend check: FY2024 was €425k, modest increase tracks headcount growth

**Combined group R&D incentive**: ~€1.84M

### Planted Error

**ERR-EU-004** (or next available EU error ID): social_charge_inclusion — In the payroll data, one CM employee (employee_id CM-R-007) has their employer social charges (cotisations patronales) entered as zero instead of the correct ~45% rate. This understates the eligible personnel cost base for that employee's qualifying projects. The correct social charge amount should be ~€31,500 (on a €70,000 gross salary). This tests whether the agent validates payroll data completeness and notices the anomaly.

Type: data_integrity — a payroll data error that propagates into the CIR computation if not caught.

### Rubric Anchors (differences from TC-08 that must appear in scoring)

1. **Frascati Manual categories (not US four-part test)**: Agent must classify CM projects using basic research / applied research / experimental development terminology. If agent applies the US four-part test (permitted purpose, technological in nature, etc.), deduct points — wrong jurisdiction.

2. **Dual-regime handling**: Agent must apply CIR rules to CM and Forschungszulage rules to CP separately. Cross-applying rules (e.g., including CP supply costs in Forschungszulage, or ignoring social charges for CIR) is an error.

3. **Personnel cost base differences**: CIR uses total employer cost (gross + social charges). Forschungszulage uses salary costs including social insurance. Agent must include employer social charges in the eligible cost base for both regimes.

4. **CIR operating expense flat rate**: Agent should apply the 50% forfait on eligible personnel costs (this is a simplification method available under CIR). If agent omits it or uses a different rate, flag.

5. **Forschungszulage personnel-only rule**: Agent must NOT include supplies, subcontracted R&D, or overhead in the German computation. If non-personnel costs appear in the Forschungszulage calculation, deduct points.

6. **Assessment basis cap**: Agent must verify whether CP's eligible personnel costs exceed the €2M cap. In this case they don't (~€1.85M), but the agent should acknowledge the cap exists.

7. **Subcontracted R&D treatment (CIR)**: Agent should correctly include the subcontracted R&D amount for CM and note the private-entity-at-cost rule (not doubled).

8. **Non-qualifying project identification**: Same adversarial structure as TC-08 — market research and routine QC/optimization must be flagged as ineligible. Agent should use European terminology for the rejection rationale (Frascati exclusions for CM, FZulG §2 exclusions for CP).

9. **Borderline project handling**: The nanocoating project at CM should be flagged for review, not definitively qualified or rejected. Agent earns points for acknowledging ambiguity.

10. **Documentation format**: CIR technical dossier (état descriptif) and FZulG BSFZ application format are distinct. Agent should not produce US-style contemporaneous documentation memos.

11. **Social charge anomaly detection**: Agent should notice the zero-social-charge employee (ERR-EU-004) and flag it as a data quality issue before including it in the computation.

### File Keys (canary prefix: tc08eu_)

- tc08eu_time_records (CSV)
- tc08eu_project_001 through tc08eu_project_012 (DOCX — 8 CM + 4 CP)
- tc08eu_payroll (XLSX)
- tc08eu_supply_expenses (XLSX)
- tc08eu_prior_year_claims (XLSX)

### Dependencies on Canonical Model

- Entity profiles from synth-data-eu.2: CM (Lyon, SAS, 250 employees, 12% R&D spend, CIR eligible) and CP (Munich, GmbH, 310 employees, 4% R&D spend, Forschungszulage eligible)
- Revenue targets: CM €32M (R&D spend ~€3.84M), CP €45M (R&D spend ~€1.8M)
- Social charge rates: France ~45% employer, Germany ~20% employer
- Corporate tax rates: France 25%, Germany 29.9% — relevant for after-tax benefit context but not for credit computation
- European standards reference: France CIR and Germany Forschungszulage entries in specs/european-standards-reference.md

### Canary & Error Count

- 17 file keys requiring canaries (1 CSV + 12 DOCX + 3 XLSX + 1 partial schedule = wait, no partial schedule here — 1 CSV + 12 DOCX + 3 XLSX = 16 keys, but prior_year_claims is the 3rd XLSX, so 1+12+3 = 16 file keys)
- Correction: tc08eu_time_records (1) + tc08eu_project_001..012 (12) + tc08eu_payroll (1) + tc08eu_supply_expenses (1) + tc08eu_prior_year_claims (1) = 16 file keys
- 1 planted error: ERR-EU-004 (social_charge_inclusion)
acceptance_criteria: Design chooses jurisdiction/regime, defines qualifying activity criteria, eligible cost categories, subcontractor/externally provided worker treatment if in scope, computation method, documentation inputs, traps for non-qualifying market research/routine testing, and rubric differences from Section 41.
close_reason: Design complete. TC-08-EU replaces US Section 41/ASC method with dual European R&D incentive regimes: France CIR (30% credit on eligible R&D spend) for CM entity and Germany Forschungszulage (25% of eligible personnel costs, max €500k) for CP entity. Key differentiators from TC-08: Frascati Manual categories instead of US four-part test, dual-entity/dual-regime structure, CIR operating expense forfait (50% of personnel), Forschungszulage personnel-only rule, subcontracted R&D treatment, €2M assessment basis cap. 12 projects (8 CM + 4 CP) with same adversarial ratio: 8 qualifying, 1 borderline, 3 non-qualifying (market research, routine QC, routine optimization). Gold standard: CIR ~€1.38M + Forschungszulage ~€462k = ~€1.84M combined. Planted error ERR-EU-004: zero social charges on one CM employee. 16 file keys with tc08eu_ prefix.

acceptance_criteria: Generated inputs open cleanly; canaries embedded; gold includes extracted investment allocations, amended-statement effects, and corporate-parent caveats; rubric anchors do not mention K-1/Form 1120/Section 199A except as US contrast in docs; self-test passes.
